applied_optimizations.name: 2D tiled kernel with coalesced memory access
applied_optimizations.description: Restructured kernel to use 2D grid (cdiv(dim2, BLOCK_M), dim0) where each program handles a tile of BLOCK_M=512 contiguous elements. This gives coalesced memory reads on every iteration of the reduction loop, making the kernel bandwidth-limited rather than launch-overhead-limited.

applied_optimizations.name: BLOCK_SIZE tuning
applied_optimizations.description: Tuned BLOCK_M from 256 to 512 for better occupancy on AMD MI355X (wavefront=64). BLOCK_M=1024 was slower due to increased register pressure.

attempted_but_failed.name: Direct strided access without permute
attempted_but_failed.reason: Strided memory access was much slower than permute+contiguous approach

attempted_but_failed.name: Loop unrolling with VEC_LOAD
attempted_but_failed.reason: Triton doesn't support break statements in loops

attempted_but_failed.name: BLOCK_M=1024
attempted_but_failed.reason: Larger block size increased register pressure and reduced performance

notes: Key insight: For reduction over middle dimension (dim=1), permuting to make reduction dimension last and using contiguous tensor gives better performance than strided access
2D grid with tiled access pattern enables coalesced memory reads which is critical for bandwidth-limited kernels
BLOCK_M=512 is optimal for this problem size on AMD MI355X
Achieved 1.41x speedup over PyTorch reference (1.48ms vs 2.08ms)
